Selkirk Snowcat Skiing: Tour Snow Quality

36 seasons of mountain weather data (1989-2026). Scores reflect best available terrain each day (guides pick optimal zone). Pick any two tours to compare.
Day-by-day detail →  |  Read the full analysis →

How the Score Works

Snow quality = best of fresh snow or preserved older snow (preserved discounted 15%).
Safety multiplies the score: great snow on dangerous terrain is worth less.
Base depth is a gate: thin base kills the experience.
Crystal quality is a minor adjustment (±10%).

Components aren't substitutable: you can't make up for zero snow with great safety. Fresh and preservation are alternatives, not additive.

Quality Tiers

90+ = Epic — stories you tell for years
80-90 = Great — classic Selkirk, what you expect when you book
70-80 = Good — solid skiing, not every run is magic
60-70 = Good enough — still worth the trip, but you notice compromises
<60 = Bad trip — you question why you came

Day Quality Thresholds

What does "safety score" mean?

Safety is calculated from 72-hour snowfall loading and warming events. It's an inverse danger score: 100 = calm and stable, 0 = extreme loading. Formula: 100 - (72h_snow_cm / 50) * 60 - max(0, max_temp_C * 5)

72h SnowMax TempSafetyWhat it means for skiing
0 cm-10C100Low danger. All terrain open, ski anywhere.
25 cm (10")-5C70Moderate. Normal cat skiing operations, full terrain.
40 cm (16")-5C52Considerable. Guides avoiding obvious avalanche paths.
50 cm (20")-3C40High danger. Steep terrain limited (0.55x multiplier), but tree skiing in sheltered zones can be excellent.
50 cm (20")+2C30High + wet slab risk. Terrain multiplier ~0.45x. Significant restrictions.
65 cm (26")0C22Extreme. Terrain multiplier ~0.25x. Guides barely operating.
83 cm+ (33")any~0Extreme. Operations likely canceled. Nobody's skiing.

Note: This is a simplified proxy. Real avalanche forecasting requires snowpack stratigraphy, weak layer history, and field observations. At a guided operation like Selkirk, the guides make terrain decisions based on far more information than loading alone.

below Good enough threshold

Data Sources & Methodology

Data Sources

SourceElevationDataPeriodRole
Open-Meteo ERA5 (reanalysis)2,529m gridDaily: temp, snowfall, wind1989-2025PRIMARY for Peak & A-Frame
BC ASWE Mt Revelstoke (2A06P)1,850mDaily: SWE, snow depth, temp1992-2011Snowfall calibration (2,053 days)
Visual CrossingCoordinatesDaily: temp, snow, wind, depth2020-2026Gap-fill for 2026
Rogers Pass + Duncan Lake549-1,330mDaily: temp, snow, precip1990-2026Lodge elevation & valley baseline
Avalanche Canada3 bandsDaily danger ratings (1-5)2012-2017, 2022-2026ML safety model training (1,100+ days)
Fidelity Mountain (2A17)1,870mMonthly: SWE, depth1963-presentSnowpack validation (396 surveys)

Elevation Model

Primary approach: Open-Meteo ERA5 reanalysis at 2,529m grid elevation provides temperature, snowfall, and wind directly at near-summit level. Only a 4.1C lapse rate correction to A-Frame Ridge (1,900m) vs the old approach's 8.8C from valley stations. This eliminates the "valley rain = mountain snow?" estimation problem.

Snowfall correction: ERA5 systematically underestimates snowfall in complex mountain terrain. Calibrated against 2,053 overlapping winter days with BC ASWE Mt Revelstoke (1,850m) observations. Non-linear correction: heavy storms ~1.7x, moderate ~2x, light ~2.8x.

Peak (2,560m)Only 31m lapse from ERA5 grid — essentially direct observation
A-Frame (1,900m)4.1C warmer than ERA5, 85% of peak snowfall
Lodge (1,280m)8.1C warmer, snowfall from valley station estimation

Precipitation is enhanced by ~8% per 100m elevation gain (orographic effect). Valley rain is converted to mountain snow when the lapse-rate-adjusted temperature is below 0C, using a temperature-dependent Snow-Liquid Ratio.

Snow Quality Score Components

ComponentWhat it measuresHow it's calculated
Fresh SnowRecent snowfall in past 48 hoursLinear scale: 0cm = 0, 60cm+ = 100
PreservationHas the snow stayed cold since it fell?Starts at 100 after snowfall. Degrades fast if max temp crosses 0C. Penalizes melt-freeze crust formation (warm then cold). Decays slowly even in cold conditions (metamorphism).
Crystal QualitySnow-Liquid Ratio at time of snowfallBased on the Nakaya morphology diagram. -12 to -18C = best dendrites (SLR 16-20:1, score 100). Near 0C = wet cement (SLR 5-8:1, score <30).
Base DepthCumulative snowpack (rocks covered?)Accumulation model: snow adds, warm days melt, daily 3% settlement. Score 100 when base exceeds threshold for elevation.
SafetyCan you ski steep terrain?Penalizes heavy 72-hour loading (storm slab risk) and warm events (wet avalanche risk). Used as a gate for "great" day classification.

Day Quality Classification

EpicScore >= 90. Multiple powder days, cold temps preserve everything, full terrain. Safety is already baked into the score as a terrain multiplier.
Great/GoodScore >= 70. Solid skiing: 1-3 powder days plus well-preserved older snow. Guides find good terrain, surfaces stay soft.
Good enoughScore >= 60. Still worth the trip. Limited fresh, some warm days, crust forming. You notice compromises but it's still cat skiing.
BadScore below 60. Rain at elevation, snowpack destroyed, or extreme danger restricting all terrain. You question why you came.

Limitations

  • No wind data in the current model. Wind breaks crystal structure and creates wind slabs. ERA5-Land reanalysis (downloading) will add this.
  • Elevation extrapolation uses a fixed lapse rate. Real conditions vary with inversions, cloud cover, and storm dynamics.
  • No direct Selkirk measurements after 1997 (when the Meadow Mountain snow course was discontinued). The model relies on proxy stations 10-90km away.
  • Snowfall estimation converts valley precipitation to mountain snow. Actual orographic enhancement varies storm-by-storm and can be 2-5x the simple 8%/100m estimate used here.
  • Avalanche hazard is a rough proxy based on loading rate and temperature. Real avalanche forecasting requires snowpack stratigraphy, weak layer tracking, and field observations.

Data Access

  • Environment Canada Historical Climate Data (bulk CSV download)
  • BC Snow Survey Data (snow pillow + manual snow course archives)
  • Open Canada: Manual Snow Survey Archive
  • Avalanche Canada Scraped Danger Ratings (2012-2017)
  • ERA5-Land Reanalysis (pending integration)
Built with data from Environment Canada, BC River Forecast Centre, and Avalanche Canada.
Source code and raw data: ~/selkirk-snow/
